text:

Montag-Donnerstag
Finkenhubelweg 11, Raum/Office F108




Kurzes Mail an andre.meichtry@bfh.ch mit Angabe:

Einheit (Fachbereich, Institut, Kompetenz-Zentrum, APP)
Forschung oder Lehre
Projekt / Thema
Stadium (Planung, Analyse, Interpretation, Publikation)






Bei Beratungen zu Bachelor- oder Master-Thesis ist es wertvoll, dass nebst den Studenten auch die jeweiligen Betreuer am Beratungsgespräch teilnehmen.




Arbeiten im Bereich der Planung, Aufbereitung, Analysen, Interpretation und Dissemination/Publikation können innerhalb eines Projektes – je nach Vereinbarung – übernommen werden.




Bereits während dem Beratungsgesprächs sollten allfällige Co-Autorenschaften geklärt werden.



Der Umgang mit Daten ist vertraulich. Verwendung der Daten für Lehre oder Forschung geschieht nur nach ausdrücklichem schriftlichen Einverständnis des Auftraggebers.




Nach einem Beratungsgespräch wird bei geplanter Zusammenarbeit in einem Projekt ein Zeitplan und ein Kostenvoranschlag erstellt. Der minimale Aufwand zur Bearbeitung eines Projekts beträgt ungefähr 30-50 Arbeitsstunden.

title: Statistik-Kolloquium am G
text:
Einmal monatlich tauschen sich Statistik-Interessierte über ausgewählte Themen zu quantitativen Methoden, Statistik und Wissenschaftsphilosophie aus. Themen können von den Teilnehmern eingebracht und auch vorgestellt werden. 
Hinweis: Im Rahmen des Kolloquiums können keine vollständigen Einführungskurse z.B. in R usw. stattfinden. Dazu sei verwiesen auf entsprechende Angebote der Hochschulen.

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Polynomial regression
Using poly() in lm():

model.cubic.poly <- lm(triceps~poly(age,3,raw=TRUE),data=triceps)
## the same model:
## model.cubic <- lm(triceps~age + I(age^2) + I(age^3),
##                   data=triceps)
tri.age.plot + 
   stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~poly(x,3,raw=T), size = 1)

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Cross-validation of different polynomials
RMSE for quadratic

library(caret)

Loading required package: lattice

set.seed(1234)
trC.lm <- trainControl(method = "repeatedcv", 
                       number = 10,         
                       repeats = 10)        
pol.model <- train(triceps ~ poly(age,3),
                       data = triceps, 
                       method = "lm",
                       trControl = trC.lm)    
pol.model$results[2]




RMSE


3.866





RMSE for different degrees

my.pol.f <- function(x){
    xx<-poly(triceps$age, x, raw=T)    
    new.data  <- cbind(triceps=triceps$triceps, xx)                                 
    pol.model <- train(triceps~., data = new.data,method = "lm")    
    RMSE.cv = pol.model$results[2]
  }

t(sapply(1:10, my.pol.f))

     RMSE  RMSE  RMSE RMSE  RMSE  RMSE  RMSE  RMSE  RMSE  RMSE 
[1,] 3.985 4.083 3.83 3.776 3.787 3.741 3.847 3.803 3.867 3.804



tri.age.plot + 
   stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~poly(x,6,raw=T), size = 1)

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Piecewise linear regression
Instead of fitting a high-degree polynomial over the entire range of \(X\), piecewise polynomial regression involves fitting separate low-degree polynomials over different regions of \(X\). For example, a piecewise cubic polynomial works by fitting a cubic regression model of the form
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3x_i^3+\epsilon_i,\]
where the coefficients differ in different parts of the range of \(X\). The points where the coefficients change are called knots \(\xi_k\), \(k=1,\dots,K\).
Let us begin with a piecewise linear (degree=1):

pred1 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age<5,]))
pred2 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age >=5 & triceps$age<10,]))
pred3 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=10 & triceps$age<20,]))
pred4 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=20 & triceps$age<30,]))
pred5 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=30 & triceps$age<40,]))
pred6 <- predict(lm(triceps~age, 
                    data = triceps[triceps$age>=40,]))
tri.age.plot + 
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age<5,], 
            aes(y = pred1, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age >=5 & triceps$age<10,], 
            aes(y = pred2, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=10 & triceps$age<20,], 
            aes(y = pred3, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=20 & triceps$age<30,], 
            aes(y = pred4, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=30 & triceps$age<40,], 
            aes(y = pred5, x=age), size = 1, col="blue") +
  geom_line(data=triceps[triceps$age>=40,], 
            aes(y = pred6, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")







Continuous piecewise linear regression
We want a continuous function. Let us define a truncated power basis function (here of degree=1) per knot \(\xi\),
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)^1_{+}=
\begin{cases}
  x-\xi, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else}.
\end{cases}
\]
The continuous piecewise regression equation is
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2 h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_6 h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]
This can be done -by hand- or with B-splines splines::bs()

pred7 <- predict(lm(triceps~ age + I((age-5)*(age>=5)) +
                                   I((age-10)*(age >= 10)) +
                                   I((age-20)*(age >= 20)) +
                                   I((age-30)*(age >= 30)) +
                                  I((age-40)*(age >= 40)),
                    data = triceps))
library(splines)
pred.lm.bs <- predict(lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40),degree=1), data=triceps))
tri.age.plot +
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.lm.bs, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")+
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred7+.2, x=age), size = 1, col="red")

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Splines
Quadratic spline
With
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)_{+}^2=
\begin{cases}
  (x-\xi)^2, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else},
\end{cases}
\]
we have as regression equation \[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3 h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_7 h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]

pred.quadsmooth <- predict(lm(triceps~ age + I(age^2) + 
                    I((age-5)^2*(age>=5)) +
                    I((age-10)^2*(age>=10)) +
                    I((age-20)^2*(age>=20)) +
                    I((age-30)^2*(age>=30)) +
                    I((age-40)^2*(age>=40)),
                    data = triceps))
pred.quadsmooth2 <- predict(lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40),degree=2), data=triceps))                        
tri.age.plot +
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.quadsmooth, x=age), size = 1, col="blue")+
  geom_line(data=triceps, 
            aes(y = pred.quadsmooth2+.2, x=age), size = 1, col="red")







Cubic Spline
Most often, cubic splines are used. Adding the following truncated power basis function per knot,
\[h(x, \xi)=(x-\xi)_{+}^3=
\begin{cases}
  (x-\xi)^3, \text{\,if\,} x>\xi\\
  0, \text{\,else},
\end{cases}
\]
to the model for a cubic polynomial will lead to a discontinuity in only the third derivative at \(\xi\); the function will remain continuous, with continuous first and second derivatives, at each of the knots:
\[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\beta_2x_i^2+\beta_3x_i^3+\beta_4h(x_i,5)+\cdots+\beta_8h(x_i,40) + \epsilon_i\]
One can show that a cubic spline has \(K+4\) parameters.
Natural Spline
Natural splines have an additional restriction that the fitted curve linear at the extremes, splines::ns()

cub.splines.bs <- lm(triceps ~ bs(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), data=triceps)
cub.splines.ns <- lm(triceps ~ ns(age, knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), data=triceps)


tri.age.plot <- ggplot(triceps, aes(x=age, y=triceps)) +
                 geom_point(alpha=0.55, color="black") + 
                 theme_minimal() 
tri.age.plot +
    stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~bs(x,knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), 
               lty = 1, col = "green") + 
    stat_smooth(method = "lm", 
               formula = y~ns(x,knots = c(5,10,20,30,40)), 
               lty = 1, col = "red")


polynomial cubic splines (green), natural cubic splines (red)

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Smoothing splines
Avoids the knot selection problem completely by using a maximal set of knots. The complexity of the fit is controlled by regularization. Problem: among all functions \(f(x)\) with two continuous derivatives, find one that minimizes the penalized residual sum of squares
\[
  RSS(f,\lambda)=\sum_{i=1}^N(y_i-f(x_i))^2+\lambda[f''(t)]^2dt
\tag{1}\]
where \(\lambda\) is a fixed smoothing parameter. The first term measures closeness to the data, while the second term penalizes curvature in the function, and \(\lambda\) establishes a tradeoff between the two. Special cases: \(\lambda=0\) (no constraint on \(f\)) and \(\lambda=\infty\) (\(f\) has to be linear).
The function \(f(x)\) that minimizes (Equation 1) can be shown to have some special properties: it is a piecewise cubic polynomial with knots at the unique values of \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) and continuous first and second derivatives at each knot. Furthermore, it is linear in the region outside of the extreme knots. In other words, the function \(f(x)\) that minimizes (Equation 1) is a natural cubic spline with knots at \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) ! However, it is not the same natural cubic spline that one would get if one applied the basis function approach described above – rather, it is a shrunken version of such a natural cubic spline, where the value of the tuning parameter \(\lambda\) in (Equation 1) controls the level of shrinkage.
Smoothing splines are implemented in smooth.spline().

\(\lambda\) determined with cross-validation

sspline <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, cv=TRUE)

Warning in smooth.spline(x = triceps$age, y = triceps$triceps, cv = TRUE): cross-validation with non-unique 'x' values seems doubtful

plot(triceps$age, triceps$triceps)
lines(sspline, lwd=3,col="red")







The extremes: no smooth and max smooth

ssplineNosmooth <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, lambda=0)
ssplineMaxsmooth <- smooth.spline(x=triceps$age, y=triceps$triceps, lambda=100)
plot(triceps$age, triceps$triceps)
lines(ssplineNosmooth, lwd=2,col="red")
lines(ssplineMaxsmooth, lwd=2,col="blue")

title: Beyond linearity
text:
Generalized additive model
Using mgcv::gam()

library(mgcv)

Loading required package: nlme


This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'.

gamtri<-gam(triceps~s(age,bs="cr"),data=triceps)
summary(gamtri)


Family: gaussian 
Link function: identity 

Formula:
triceps ~ s(age, bs = "cr")

Parametric coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)    9.702      0.126    77.3   <2e-16

Approximate significance of smooth terms:
        edf Ref.df    F p-value
s(age) 6.71   7.54 85.6  <2e-16

R-sq.(adj) =  0.419   Deviance explained = 42.3%
GCV =  14.18  Scale est. = 14.057    n = 892

plot(gamtri)

title: “All Is Integration”
text:
Statistics, viewed from a sufficient distance, is often presented as a science of estimation, testing, prediction, or uncertainty quantification. Yet beneath these familiar activities lies a deeper and more universal mathematical structure: the repeated necessity of integrating over what cannot be directly observed.












Model class
Example model
Observation model
What is integrated over?
Integral / summation structure
Philosophical interpretation
Additional explanation




Deterministic regression
\(y=x^\top\beta\)
none
nothing
none
Pure functional law
No probability model; data assumed to follow an exact deterministic relation.


Gaussian LM
\(Y_i=x_i^\top\beta+\varepsilon_i\)
\(\varepsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\)
Gaussian error noise
implicit in Gaussian density
Additive uncertainty around deterministic mean
Classical regression with homoscedastic additive residuals.


One-sample \(t\)-test
\(Y_i\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\)
Gaussian
error variability
product of normal densities
Mean inference under stochastic sampling
Special case of Gaussian likelihood with one mean parameter.


Two-sample \(t\)-test
\(Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1G_i+\varepsilon_i\)
Gaussian
residual noise
Gaussian likelihood
Group differences with uncertainty
Equivalent to a linear model with a binary group predictor.


ANOVA
\(Y_{ij}=\mu+\alpha_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\)
Gaussian
residual noise
Gaussian likelihood
Group means plus stochastic variation
Multi-group extension of the linear model.


Logistic regression
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p_i)\)
Bernoulli
binary outcome randomness
product of Bernoulli probabilities
Uncertainty in binary realizations
Models probabilities through a link function such as the logit.


Poisson regression
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\mu_i)\)
Poisson
count randomness
product of Poisson probabilities
Event generation process
Variance equals the mean under the Poisson assumption.


Gamma GLM
\(Y_i\sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\mu_i,\phi)\)
Gamma
multiplicative positive noise
Gamma likelihood
Relative / proportional variability
Suitable for positive skewed outcomes with variance increasing quadratically with the mean.


Negative-binomial GLM
\(Y_i\sim NB(\mu_i,\theta)\)
NB
Poisson intensity heterogeneity
Poisson-Gamma mixture
Extra latent variability in rates
Can be derived as a Poisson model with a Gamma-distributed latent rate.


Probit regression
\(Y_i=1[Z_i>0]\)
latent Gaussian threshold
latent \(Z_i\)
\(\int 1(z>0)\phi(z)\,dz\)
Hidden continuous propensity
Binary outcomes arise from an unobserved continuous latent variable.


Finite mixture model
\(Y_i\sim \sum_k \pi_k f_k(y)\)
mixture
latent class membership
\(\sum_k \pi_k f_k(y)\)
Population heterogeneity
Data assumed to arise from several overlapping subpopulations.


Latent class analysis
categorical latent groups
multinomial
latent class \(C_i\)
\(\sum_c P(Y\mid C=c)P(C=c)\)
Hidden subpopulations
Common in psychometrics and sociology for identifying hidden categories.


Factor analysis
\(Y=\Lambda f+\varepsilon\)
Gaussian
latent factors \(f\)
\(\int f(y\mid f)p(f)df\)
Hidden dimensions generate observed variables
Correlations among variables explained through lower-dimensional latent traits.


IRT / Rasch
\(P(Y_{ij}=1)=\mathrm{logit}^{-1}(\theta_i-b_j)\)
Bernoulli
latent ability \(\theta_i\)
\(\int P(Y\mid\theta)p(\theta)d\theta\)
Hidden trait generates responses
Item responses modeled via latent ability and item difficulty.


Random intercept LMM
\(Y_{ij}=x_{ij}^\top\beta+b_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\)
Gaussian
random intercepts \(b_j\)
\(\int f(y\mid b)f(b)db\)
Cluster heterogeneity
Observations within a cluster become correlated through shared latent effects.


Random slope model
\(Y_{ij}=x_{ij}^\top\beta+b_{1j}+b_{2j}x_{ij}+\varepsilon\)
Gaussian
random coefficient vector
multivariate integral
Individual-specific effects
Allows regression slopes to vary between clusters or persons.


Logistic GLMM
binary mixed model
Bernoulli
random effects
\(\int f(y\mid b)f(b)db\)
Cluster-specific risk differences
Extends logistic regression to clustered or longitudinal data.


Poisson GLMM
count mixed model
Poisson
latent cluster intensity
\(\int f(y\mid b)f(b)db\)
Unobserved rate heterogeneity
Random effects induce extra-Poisson variability and correlation.


Gamma GLMM
positive skewed mixed model
Gamma
latent cluster effects
\(\int f(y\mid b)f(b)db\)
Multiplicative cluster heterogeneity
Useful for repeated positive skewed outcomes.


Frailty survival model
hazard with frailty \(u_j\)
survival process
frailty term
\(\int f(t\mid u)f(u)du\)
Hidden susceptibility
Survival differences explained by latent vulnerability.


Hidden Markov model
latent states \(S_t\)
conditional observation model
hidden states
\(\sum_{S_1,\dots,S_T}\)
Hidden dynamic process
Observed sequences generated from unobserved evolving states.


Kalman filter
state-space Gaussian model
Gaussian
latent states
recursive Gaussian integration
Continuous hidden dynamics
Sequential updating of hidden continuous states over time.


Gaussian process regression
\(f(x)\sim GP\)
Gaussian
latent function \(f\)
functional integral
Distribution over functions
Bayesian nonparametric regression over infinite-dimensional function spaces.


Ridge regression
penalized LM
Gaussian
equivalent Gaussian prior on \(\beta\)
MAP / Bayesian integral
Shrinkage via prior uncertainty
Penalization corresponds to Gaussian priors on coefficients.


Lasso
Laplace prior
Gaussian
prior uncertainty on coefficients
Bayesian interpretation
Sparse latent structure
Encourages sparse solutions by shrinking some coefficients exactly to zero.


Bayesian linear regression
prior on \(\beta,\sigma^2\)
Gaussian
parameter uncertainty
\(\int f(y\mid\theta)p(\theta)d\theta\)
Epistemic uncertainty
Uncertainty exists not only in observations but also in parameters.


Bayesian GLM
prior on coefficients
exponential-family
posterior over parameters
full posterior integral
Uncertainty in effects
Generalizes Bayesian inference to non-Gaussian outcomes.


Bayesian GLMM
priors + random effects
hierarchical
parameters and random effects
nested integrals
Full uncertainty hierarchy
Simultaneously integrates over latent effects and parameter uncertainty.


Posterior predictive distribution
\(p(y^{new}\mid y)\)
predictive
posterior uncertainty
\(\int p(y^{new}\mid\theta)p(\theta\mid y)d\theta\)
Prediction averaged over uncertainty
Bayesian prediction averages over all plausible parameter values.


Marginal likelihood
\(p(y)\)
any Bayesian model
all parameter uncertainty
\(\int f(y\mid\theta)p(\theta)d\theta\)
Occam averaging
Central quantity in Bayesian model comparison and Bayes factors.


EM algorithm
incomplete-data likelihood
latent-variable model
latent missing data
\(\int f(y,z\mid\theta)dz\)
Unobserved structure
Alternates between estimating latent variables and parameters.


Missing-data model
observed-data likelihood
partial observation
missing values
\(\int f(y_{obs},y_{mis})dy_{mis}\)
Uncertainty about missingness
Missing values treated as latent unknown quantities.


Causal marginalization
potential outcomes
counterfactual model
covariate distribution
\(\int E(Y\mid X=x)dF(x)\)
Population-average causal effect
Standardization averages causal effects over a target population.


Marginal means / emmeans
adjusted means
regression model
nuisance covariates
averaging over covariate distribution
Population-adjusted effects
Produces covariate-adjusted group means or contrasts.


Random-effects meta-analysis
study effects \(\theta_j\sim N(\mu,\tau^2)\)
study likelihoods
true study heterogeneity
\(\int f(y_j\mid\theta_j)f(\theta_j)d\theta_j\)
Between-study variability
Studies assumed to estimate varying underlying true effects.


Deep neural network (Bayesian view)
stochastic weights
flexible nonlinear model
parameter uncertainty
high-dimensional integral
Distribution over representations
Bayesian interpretation treats weights as uncertain latent parameters.


Variational Bayes
approximate posterior
arbitrary
latent posterior distribution
optimized approximate integral
Tractable uncertainty approximation
Replaces exact integration by optimization over simpler distributions.


MCMC
sampling algorithm
arbitrary Bayesian model
posterior distribution
Monte Carlo integration
Numerical integration engine
Numerical approximation of otherwise intractable integrals.

title: Compositions
text:
Sample space of compositional data
The sample space of compositional data with \(D\) parts is a simplex \(\mathcal{S}^D\) of dimension \(D-1\):
\(\mathcal{S}^D=\{\mathbf{x}=[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_D]\in \mathbb{R}^D \mid
x_i>0, i=1,2,\dots,D; \sum_{i=1}^Dx_i=\mathcal{k}\}\)
The only information is given by the ratios between components, so the information of a composition is preserved under multiplication by any positive constant. Therefore, the sample space of compositional data can always be assumed to be a standard simplex, i.e. \(\mathcal{k}
= 1\).
Normalization to the standard simplex is called closure and is
\(\mathcal{C}[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_D]=\left[\frac{x_1}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i},\frac{x_2}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i}, \dots,\frac{x_D}{\sum_{i=1}^D x_i}\right]\)
Example
4 Raters rated a 4-part-composition on \(n=20\) subjects. See the interactive Tetrahedron

plot3D(datAitch,col=as.numeric(Rater),size=5,axes=TRUE,coors=TRUE)

title: Das Anti-Falsifikations-Paradigma
text:
Karl Popper kritisierte erkenntnistheoretische Positionen, die die Existenz einer objektiven Realität, die Möglichkeit objektiver Erkenntnis, rationale Kritik oder wissenschaftliche Prüfbarkeit relativieren. Er verstand sich ausdrücklich als Verteidiger der Aufklärung: Vernunft, offene Kritik, argumentative Auseinandersetzung und die Möglichkeit objektiver Erkenntnis standen im Zentrum seines Denkens. Positionen, die wissenschaftliche Erkenntnis primär als soziale Konstruktion, Machtprodukt oder Perspektivenfrage interpretieren, gefährden aus seiner Sicht die rationale Kritik und fördern einen erkenntnistheoretischen Relativismus. Wissenschaft macht nach Popper gerade deshalb Fortschritte, weil Theorien an einer von uns unabhängigen Realität scheitern können. Werden dagegen alle Sichtweisen primär als „konstruiert“ betrachtet, wird die Unterscheidung zwischen besseren und schlechteren Erklärungen erschwert. Darin sah Popper eine problematische Abkehr vom aufklärerischen Ideal der kritischen Rationalität. Selbst Entwicklungen der modernen Physik, etwa die Quantenmechanik mit ihren kontraintuitiven Phänomenen, verstand Popper nicht als Argument gegen die Existenz einer objektiven Realität. Dass wissenschaftliche Theorien immer wieder durch Beobachtungen und Experimente korrigiert werden müssen, wertete er vielmehr als Hinweis darauf, dass die Welt unabhängig von unseren Überzeugungen existiert und unseren Konstruktionen Grenzen setzt. Diese Sicht steht im deutlichen Gegensatz zu erkenntnistheoretischen Positionen, die wissenschaftliches Wissen primär als Ergebnis sozialer Aushandlungsprozesse verstehen*.

Dazu mein Beitrag zum wissenschaftlichen Diskurs in den Gesundheitswissenschaften: Das Anti-Falsifikations-Paradigma
